questions.text: I think that the ability to process painful experiences through a rational framework is what makes it possible to provide sustained help without burning out.
I believe the intensity of my inner life is something most people would find surprising.
I pick up on subtle changes in how someone addresses me that signal a shift in our relationship.
I believe that the pull to act on behalf of those who are suffering is among the most ethically sound motivations a person can follow.
I find social situations easier when expectations are made explicit.
I organize social events spontaneously, often on the same day they happen.
I schedule appointments with buffer time between them to avoid feeling rushed.
I experience love as an almost overwhelming force rather than a steady warmth.
I tend to negotiate the arrangement that works best for me in any shared situation.
I often take the initiative to resolve ambiguity for others.
I often help synthesize multiple perspectives into a direction the group can support.
I believe that self-denial for the sake of others is deeply meaningful.
I ask people what they would like to take on rather than assigning tasks.
My emotional responses are like a well-tuned instrument — responsive but never distorted.
I feel that guarding my creative energy from others' demands is essential to producing my best work.
I tend to feel the same emotional temperature whether I'm at home or in a novel situation.
I hold the view that generosity is not about what you have but about how much you're willing to let go of.
I tend to be unaware of when someone is jealous but hiding it.
I think that structured, analytical engagement with emotionally difficult situations is the approach most likely to produce solutions that stand the test of time.
I tend to be unaware of power dynamics playing out in conversations.
I feel that the weight of suffering in my moral consciousness is the feature that makes my ethical outlook genuinely responsive to the conditions of real human lives.
I find that my emotional responses are like a well-regulated thermostat — they adjust smoothly without overshooting.
I detect when someone is watching a conversation from the periphery with more interest than they are showing.
I can tell from someone's greeting whether they are having a good or bad day.
I am energized by pitching my vision to stakeholders.
I prefer that new team members be selected by the whole team together.
I feel the strongest moral pull to help the person who is most vulnerable in a group.
I treat my future obligations as commitments that deserve present-day preparation.
I believe that I should be my own first priority in life.
I tend to search for important documents when I need them rather than filing them systematically.
I feel that establishing hierarchy early prevents confusion later.
I batch similar tasks together to work more efficiently.
I find that I am happiest when I have nothing left because I gave it all away.
My emotional responses have a vividness that makes everyday life feel dramatic.
I believe that inspiration and intuition are more reliable guides than any process.
I believe that the discipline of objective evaluation during emotionally fraught moments is what separates considered judgment from mere reaction.
